label: Brand or company
placeholder: Example: Creatornivo
helper: Enter the brand, company, project, or creator name behind the offer.
why: It identifies who is making the offer and supports accurate brand references.

label: Offer name
placeholder: Example: AI Content Template Library
helper: Name the product, service, application, event, resource, or campaign.
why: The landing page must clearly identify what is being presented.

label: Offer type
placeholder: Select the closest offer type
helper: This changes the page structure, terminology, proof needs, and conversion flow.
why: Different offer types require different explanations, sections, and calls to action.

label: Primary page goal
placeholder: Example: Get visitors to start a free trial
helper: Describe the single most important action or result for this page.
why: The goal determines the message hierarchy and dominant CTA.

label: Target audience
placeholder: Example: Solo creators who need faster content production without hiring a team
helper: Describe the people or organizations this offer is specifically for.
why: Audience details materially affect language, benefits, objections, and positioning.

label: Core problem
placeholder: What frustrating, costly, slow, or difficult situation does the offer address?
helper: Focus on the main problem the visitor already recognizes or experiences.
why: The page needs an accurate problem context without inventing audience pain points.

label: Main value proposition
placeholder: Explain the main value or outcome without making an unsupported guarantee
helper: State why the offer matters and what useful change it can help create.
why: It provides the central promise around which the page is structured.

label: Primary CTA
placeholder: Example: Start Free Trial
helper: Leave as Auto to generate the clearest action for the selected goal.
why: It lets the user control the exact primary action when required.

label: CTA destination URL
placeholder: https://example.com/signup
helper: Add the page, form, checkout, booking, download, or signup destination.
why: It helps keep CTA references consistent with the real conversion destination.

label: Output language
placeholder: Example: English, Ukrainian, Polish
helper: Enter the language in which the landing page should be written.
why: A free-text language field supports multilingual output without a restrictive language list.

label: Page depth
placeholder: Choose the preferred page depth
helper: Auto selects the shortest depth that explains the offer and handles key objections.
why: It controls content depth without asking the user for a technical word count.

label: Audience context
placeholder: Describe their situation, priorities, experience, or buying context
helper: Add details that affect how the audience evaluates this offer.
why: It enables more precise messaging without splitting audience information across several minor fields.

label: Desired audience outcome
placeholder: What does the audience want to achieve, improve, avoid, or simplify?
helper: Describe the practical outcome from the audience’s perspective.
why: It distinguishes the audience’s desired result from the company’s conversion goal.

label: Key differentiators
placeholder: What makes this offer meaningfully different from common alternatives?
helper: Use specific, supportable differences rather than broad superiority claims.
why: Specific differentiators materially improve positioning and conversion clarity.

label: Audience awareness
placeholder: How familiar is the audience?
helper: Auto infers the appropriate explanation level from the other inputs.
why: Awareness level changes whether the page should educate, differentiate, or focus on action.

label: Main objections
placeholder: Example: Price, setup time, trust, compatibility, effort, or switching concerns
helper: List the genuine reasons a suitable visitor may hesitate.
why: Objection handling is central to landing-page performance and cannot always be inferred safely.

label: Alternatives or competitors
placeholder: Describe current solutions, manual methods, or named competitors when relevant
helper: Used only for accurate positioning, not unsupported attacks or comparisons.
why: It clarifies the visitor’s current options and the offer’s place among them.

label: Key benefits
placeholder: List the most important practical benefits for the customer
helper: Focus on useful outcomes rather than broad promotional claims.
why: Supplied benefits prevent the model from inferring unsupported customer outcomes.

label: Features or deliverables
placeholder: List included features, services, files, sessions, modules, or deliverables
helper: Include only items that are currently available or confirmed.
why: The page must accurately explain what the visitor receives.

label: Pricing and terms
placeholder: Add verified pricing, billing, trial, refund, eligibility, or contract details
helper: Leave blank when pricing should not appear or has not been finalized.
why: Commercial details must come from the user and must not be fabricated.

label: Conversion path
placeholder: What happens after the CTA?
helper: This controls button wording, friction level, process explanation, and privacy context.
why: The action after the click determines how the page should set expectations.

label: Secondary CTA
placeholder: Example: View Demo
helper: Use only when visitors need a lower-commitment alternative.
why: Some landing pages benefit from one subordinate action, but it should not be generated automatically in every case.

label: Time-sensitive offer
placeholder: Not applicable
helper: Enable only when a real deadline, quantity, eligibility rule, or capacity limit exists.
why: It prevents routine or fabricated urgency from being added to the page.

label: Deadline or availability
placeholder: State the exact deadline, quantity, capacity, eligibility, and relevant conditions
helper: Only verified and concrete limitations may appear in the generated copy.
why: Real urgency requires factual details that the model cannot infer.

label: Brand voice
placeholder: Choose the closest voice
helper: Select the general voice; add finer details in Brand style notes.
why: A simple voice selection changes the wording without creating an excessive style questionnaire.

label: Brand style notes
placeholder: Add preferred wording, terminology, examples, or brand phrases to avoid
helper: Use this for brand-specific guidance that cannot be inferred safely.
why: It supports real brand consistency beyond a generic tone preset.

label: Verified proof available
placeholder: Not applicable
helper: Enable when you can supply testimonials, results, statistics, clients, awards, or certifications.
why: It prevents the automatic generation of fabricated trust signals.

label: Proof details
placeholder: Paste approved testimonials, statistics, results, credentials, or attribution details
helper: Include exact wording, numbers, sources, dates, qualifiers, and permissions where relevant.
why: Proof must be supplied accurately and preserved without invention or material distortion.

label: Regulated topic
placeholder: Select only when relevant
helper: This activates more cautious claim, advice, and disclosure handling.
why: High-stakes subjects require different claim standards and may depend on jurisdiction.

label: Relevant jurisdiction
placeholder: Example: United States, European Union, Ukraine, California
helper: Enter the country, region, or market whose rules may affect the copy.
why: Legal and compliance language can vary materially between jurisdictions.

label: Restrictions and disclosures
placeholder: Add mandatory wording, claim limits, exclusions, eligibility rules, or prohibited statements
helper: Combine all required restrictions and disclosures in one place.
why: It preserves material conditions while avoiding several overlapping compliance fields.

label: Privacy and data notes
placeholder: Explain what data is collected, why, consent wording, or relevant privacy conditions
helper: Include only confirmed data practices and approved privacy wording.
why: Conversion flows that collect personal data may require accurate expectation and consent language.

label: Primary SEO keyword
placeholder: Example: AI content templates
helper: Add one realistic search phrase when organic search matters.
why: It provides search-intent direction without requiring a full SEO questionnaire.

label: Supporting keywords
placeholder: Add a short list of closely related phrases, separated by commas
helper: Use only relevant phrases; the generator will not force every term into the copy.
why: Related phrases can improve topic coverage while remaining optional.

label: FAQ section
placeholder: Choose whether to include FAQs
helper: Auto adds FAQs only when they help resolve meaningful visitor uncertainty.
why: A FAQ is useful for some offers but should not be forced onto every landing page.

label: Additional context
placeholder: Add any remaining implementation requirements or essential context
helper: Use this single field for important information not covered elsewhere.
why: It handles legitimate edge cases without creating several miscellaneous fields.
